taintFlow: TaintFlow{<vulnerable.example.app.MainActivity: void onCreate(android.os.Bundle)>[4@L21] $r2 = invokevirtual %this.getIntent()/result -> <vulnerable.example.app.WebViewActivity: void onCreate(android.os.Bundle)>[11@L26] invokevirtual r3.loadUrl($r5)/0}
llmVerdict:
- Source: MainActivity reads attacker-controlled Intent data (getIntent()) and processes a deep link if action is VIEW and data URI exists.
- Routing: processDeeplink() accepts URIs with scheme "custom", host "deeplink", and path "/webview". It extracts the "url" query parameter and only checks that Uri.parse(url).getHost() != null.
- Sink: The value is placed into an Intent extra "url" and passed to WebViewActivity, which directly calls webView.loadUrl(getIntent().getStringExtra("url")) with JavaScript enabled and allowFileAccessFromFileURLs(true).
- Exposure: MainActivity is exported="true". Even though there’s no VIEW intent-filter, an external app can start it explicitly (-n) with action VIEW and the crafted data URI. This satisfies the app’s checks and leads to loading an attacker-controlled arbitrary http/https URL.
- No allowlisting or robust validation (e.g., scheme/host restriction to trusted domains) is present. Therefore, the exact source-to-sink path is exploitable as-is.

Impact: An attacker can force the app’s WebView to load arbitrary web content with JavaScript enabled, which can facilitate phishing, arbitrary JS execution in the app’s WebView context, and potential abuse of relaxed file access settings.

taintFlow: TaintFlow{<vulnerable.example.app.MainActivity: void onCreate(android.os.Bundle)>[11@L22] $r5 = invokevirtual $r2.getData()/result -> <vulnerable.example.app.WebViewActivity: void onCreate(android.os.Bundle)>[11@L26] invokevirtual r3.loadUrl($r5)/0}
llmVerdict:
- Source: MainActivity receives an explicit external Intent with action VIEW and data Uri via intent.getData().
- Reachability: MainActivity is exported (android:exported="true") and, although it has no VIEW intent-filter, it can be explicitly started by external apps (or adb) with action VIEW and data.
- Processing: processDeeplink() checks only that scheme == "custom", host == "deeplink", path == "/webview", and that the query parameter "url" has a non-null host (Uri.parse(url).getHost() != null). No domain allowlist or scheme restriction is enforced.
- Sink: WebViewActivity enables JavaScript and allowFileAccessFromFileURLs, then directly calls webView.loadUrl(getIntent().getStringExtra("url")).
- Result: An attacker can supply any http/https URL (or any scheme with a host, e.g., content) through the deep link and have it loaded in a WebView with JavaScript enabled. This satisfies the WebView Vulnerability pattern: attacker-controlled data reaches WebView.loadUrl without proper validation.

Therefore, the provided flow is exploitable as-is.
